sentences.text: The first persons they encountered were the three of us standing in a row with me in the middle.
They were coming in from the left somehow.
And all I remember is the suits and overcoats, revolver, and the green hats, clean-shaven people.
And one of them said, your name, meaning-- in German many things are commands instead of sentences.
America is such a polite country and a polite language.
German is not such a polite language.
It commands a lot of things.
Your name-- and the meaning was my father was to state his name or our name.
He wouldn't do it.
This repeated itself louder two or three more times.
By the third time, the one man hauled off and just landed him a resounding slap in the face, to the point that his glasses fell off.
Like me, he needs his glasses, very thick glasses.
And I see them on the floor.
And, you know, the first instinct, you're trained as a child that you jump when something goes wrong.
You were supposed to retrieve that.
Young people bend down and get that.
But I could feel my parents were holding me in place, and we just stood.
The reason he didn't say his name was he knew we were cooked if he said Goldstein.
Because over there-- again, just in contrast to here, the minute you heard that name, you knew, of course, that that was a Jewish name, as Jewish as they get.
It wasn't a name like Schmidt that could be either way or Brown that could be either way.
Uh-uh.
Well, about that time-- and this was prearranged between Mr.
Levent and my father.
Now, mind you, even in hiding they called each other Levent and Goldstein to each other, because you just don't first name people.
Even in this intimate, crazy, dangerous circumstance, there were no first names used.
And the agreement was, one night, I guess, he had told my father, if they should break in here-- this was in the dark- you three make a run for it, because it's really too late for us.
Forget the postal uniforms.
Forget the roof.
This is not going to work.
That was a dream four years ago that might work.
Well, apparently, this was the moment that signaled to Levent it was time for his act.
We didn't know what his act was going to be.
His act was that he and his wife would pretend, in their small room without a door or a window, to get away.
Can you believe this?
Pretending to get away.
And one of them-- of the plainclothesmen shouted, it's Levent.
He's trying to get away.
And so this one and the other one who was standing nearby-- two must have been in the back with them-- he must have made a scene enough that they thought he would escape them.
Rushed through that door, that connecting door, to that area of the scuffle, and my father knew that was his signal.
And he pulled a key out of his pocket.
He always had, apparently, this key on him.
Each had a key on him, in case of an emergency, to lock that door between.
And he locked that door between, in essence locking the Gestapo in with the two Levents.
And we-- out through the hole.

And on the-- in the corridor of that attic, my-- when my mother and father went through that-- and of course, we were covered with soot from that ash and debris there that had been piled up against there.
As I was coming out of there sort of haltingly, that man, who was probably just somebody doing a repair there-- it wasn't Maletski.
He had evaporated.
His role had been fulfilled, and his reward would be forthcoming surely.
This other little man said, now, [NON-ENGLISH], just sort of stunned to see a child emerging from this hole, which meant, well, come on out, little one.
He couldn't believe what he was seeing.
But you see, he could have had he  been made of the same stuff as Mr.
Maletski, said, here they are making a run for it.
It took some time for us to find out that they were not even looking for us yet.
We were caught in a situation where it was the Levents that they were looking for.
They were not even searching for us just yet.
We would have just been caught up in the web.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The man did nothing and just walked away, fortunately.
So there you have a case of people being good and innocent maybe surprised had no ax to grind with anyone.

My father picked me up, and they ran for the nearest elevator-- there was an elevator that came right up to there-- instead of going through all those-- the square with the four doors, which still would lead you down into the courtyard.
And we went on the elevator, and I remember being carried and hurrying, always hurrying, stairs, this, that.
It was the same on attic day.
When we went into the attic, we tore up the stairs just ahead of the Gestapo, and the Levents pulled us in into the area that would get us to there.
We came outside through a gate through which the postal wagons would drive on their routes.
And there, in front of the building-- and my father kept saying walk calmly.
We are just out for a walk.
You couldn't look like someone who was getting away.
He said, just walk calmly.

And I'm between my parents-- I still remember this-- we get out in front, and there were-- I don't remember, two, three trucks, kind of like army trucks you have to think, loaded with people standing.
Now, the danger was that since we had lived there for some time, you shopped in your immediate neighborhood.
So did maybe other people.

It was risky that someone might, hoping to get some personal advantage even out of this-- this happened also, you don't know what people do, or I'm caught, why are they walking-- could have shouted out, there they go, the Goldsteins, you know.
But this did not happen, and they walked calmly.
So we really left with the clothes we had on.
I mean, in the moments before they broke in we put on coats, you know.
We had the good sense to do that.
No, nothing, nothing.
And we walked calmly to the corner.
And that was another case of-- my job was not to step on the lines of these squares, you know, cement squares.
And we picked up speed toward the end of that long, long block that was never going to end, past the kindergarten and all.
And we caught the first streetcar that came.
And of course, this was downtown.
We lived downtown, which was a blessing.
There was always some public vehicle coming or going, primarily streetcars.
And we hopped on.
My father was a-- started right away with his little number.
We didn't have any money to pay for our fares.
What kind of people wouldn't have money to pay for fares?
So he left.
He said, you know, I have a silver knife here.
Can I give you that, and you turn it in at the barnyard for the streetcars, and I'll be down tomorrow and I'll get it back and pay the fares?
Well, he knew nobody was going to take anything to the barnyard because people were confiscating what they could to turn into usable things.
And that's how we embarked on this way to the-- on our way to the colleague, the man, the German man.
He knew of nobody else to go to.
Somebody he sold goods to that he trusted, simply because it had been years.
And he was selling to him even though it was forbidden.
So that-- it's all he could do.
True, he had another option.

He had a business card-- and I have a copy of that original business card, I mean, I have another one, I received it from the person later-- a business card given to him in the greengrocer's store while he was picking up his lousy little ration that was allowed us at one time.
The young woman who was standing next to him said, say, if you ever need a rented room, feel free to call me.

Because the woman behind the counter, the owner of that greengrocery, was giving him some things that she actually was forbidden by law to give him, and he was very, very nervous and upset because now this young woman had witnessed this.
This could get the greengrocery in trouble, as well as him.
And when the young woman left, it turned out that was her niece, was a university student.
I think it was the Humboldt University in Berlin.
And he had that card, but I don't think he had made the connection yet.
The emergency had all happened differently.
He never got to use the ax he kept by his front door where we last lived before the attic.
Because he had always said, if they come in here for us, one of them is going with me.
I'll whack him in the head with the ax.
And I remember the ax standing there.
It was just standing there without a cover or anything.
And somehow, it made him secure, you know.
Nothing came as he thought it would.
And it hadn't occurred to him yet that he could call on these, that these might be OK people.
This was not a trap or a come-on or anything of the kind.
So that was the beginning of our being on our own.
Well, we had to get some semblance of order to what we were doing.
And I remember we stopped at my grandfather's apartment.
Grandfather, my mother's sister, and the husband lived in the same apartment and had since the marriage of the sister the year before.
That way they were able to stay living there and didn't have to have other strange people in there because it wasn't a big place.
My mother had a key to that place, and we went there.
That was another place we spent one night.
But again, it was dangerous.
The Gestapo might come.
They might-- would put the seal over the door, all these things.
It was dangerous.
We had to leave that by the next day.
It was not a place to stay.
And then somehow they made contact with a student whose name was at that time Hildegard Knies, now Arnold, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] who somehow put my mother in touch and had her meet Dr.

Elisabeth Abegg, who was a philosophy professor, oh, probably in her 60s by then, who was one of those early people removed from their formal teaching posts and demoted because it was the wrong philosophy.
And the students, of course, that she had were still clamoring for what she had to say, and so there was a tiny network of students.
And Dr.
Abegg and Dr.
Abegg's hunchback sister, whom she supported-- this was a case of having a childhood injury, fell-- the bathtub fell off the counter with her in it.
I mean, she was two years old.
And she was extremely hunchbacked.
She did all the sewing.
She did sewing for people in distress-- pajamas, nightgowns, anything that she could make.
Taking old stuff apart to make something new out of.
That's all you could do.
And Dr.
Abegg was really the brains behind all the planning.
I remember that we had to split up.
That was the end of our being together, living together in any way.
Not that I know of.
No, not the colleague, through the greengrocer woman.
The colleague was somebody that my father had sold cigars to well into the time when it wasn't allowed anymore.
That was just a one-night stop.
He said, I cannot run this risk.
And three people, imagine, and there's my father without the uniform again.
This was a primary problem.

And the other problem is, if you got stopped and you just said, I don't have my papers, but I'm a soldier on furlough, which he did later on for a time, if they're suspicious of you all they had to say was, drop your pants.
Remember that German people in those decades were not circumcised.
So that was a clear identification, a clear way for people-- for men to run into problems.
I remember that Hildegard Arnold took me home with her that-- at that time right from the first meeting so that they wouldn't have to worry about lugging a small child around.
Right then and there, yeah.
Well--
Yeah, yeah.
No, no.
There was no thought.
You know, you look at your parent, and if your parent says, you go with this nice Aunt Hilde, hey, you trust that.
In fact, my husband has said to this day he's never met anyone more trusting than I am.
How can it be that I lived through these events, all of them, and I'm so trusting that it's almost childish?
It's almost a childish trust.
And I said, well, I don't know.
We have to believe in something.
So you went with--
I went with Aunt Hilde, Tante Hilde, who happened to have a very nice, gentle, soft-spoken personality, never had children of her own.
She was a year younger than my mother.
Took me to her very nice apartment, which was financed by her dad who was a high-ranking Nazi.
His subsequent children from the second wife probably wouldn't like hearing that on this tape, but c'est la vie.
He was.
And he gave her things like a leather-bound and he-- she was to display it on a very elegant bookcase.
And she would, as long as he was coming over.
And as soon as he had left-- bingo, right into the drawer again.
So it was a defiance.

And I even wondered whether a lot of her helping people like us was defiance against her father and the fact that he had married a woman close to her own age and had, I think, one or two subsequent children.
Could have been.
And he made it up to her through money.
She used that money.
She used that money on people like us.
Now, at this point already, did you have a different name?
Well, yes.
And I don't remember-- that started before we even went underground.
Or it may have started during the days before, that my father said, and my mother, your name is really Eva Holstein, not Evy Goldstein.
You haven't heard this correctly.
And I said, oh, I know my own name.
Mm-mm.
I saw that as a game they were trying to play.
But that was the beginning of the first lie.
Now remember, your training-- I lived in a society and in a culture where it's unthinkable.
The things that my children eventually lied about simply would not cross my mind.
It just-- I can't understand it.
And finally, my father just ordered and he said, that is what you're to answer if someone asks you what your name is.
There was no use trying the misunderstanding thing.
And so it was, you know.
And they would use little opportunities so that I could use that, and it was just as well they had done that.
We got to Hilde Arnold's.
And I still remember this as clearly as could be.
She had some round club chairs, real round, that you sat in, very comfortable, very expensive, very nice covering, wool covering.
And she said, now we're going to make you a bed.
And there was someone else there.
I don't know who that was.
It could have been a friend of hers who also was instrumental and much help, and her nickname was [GERMAN].
I haven't had contact with her.
I went to see her once in Cologne since I've been in America but somehow slipped by.
But she was also a significant helper.
Probably just before five, where-- where I still haven't had the birthday yet.
Yeah, she pulled the two chairs together.
I still remember that.
And she said, isn't this going to be a wonderful bed, as she pulled the two together, and they looked oval sort of.
I've always liked oval shapes probably since then.
Fixed up a little makeshift bed with sheets and the whole nine yards and she said, this will be yours.
And of course, we had the clothes we had on.
She said, well, we don't have anything for a nightgown.
And in Europe, it's not thinkable that you would not wear a nightgown.
This is all another time and place of propriety.
But we made do.
And I remember going to sleep in there and thinking what a fun thing.
It was the way people might think of a fort or a hide-- a hideout that feels safe around you because these things were high, you know, and I'm down in here.
And must have stayed there several days before I moved on.
I moved on-- oh, I was also told-- I forgot this.
I was told-- when I said, why do we have to be here in the attic, I was told that the house was bombed out.
Now, I wasn't old enough to understand that this didn't make sense.
We're in the attic of our house, but the house was bombed out.
Well, they kept practicing that.
Yes, we're bombed out.
The answer would be, why are you living wherever you are?
You know, strangers on a train would engage people in conversation that they were suspicious of.
Tante Hilde took me, kept me several days, and then would drop me at a friend's, this friend [GERMAN], who had parents with a Nazi father, but the mother was very nice.
She would take me grocery shopping with her on the days I was there, because you shopped every day because you had no refrigeration.
And I remember she happened to shop in the neighborhood where we lived.
And we're walking along and I said, well, look at this.
Here's our apartment building.
This isn't bombed out.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And this woman got frightened probably right down to her toes.
Forget the shopping.
We went home.
She called her daughter in and said, get rid of this child.
There is something wrong with this child.
We can't have it.
If your father finds out there's something wrong with this child.
We cannot do it.
These are the same people at whose dinner table I remember sitting.
And dinner there is during the day, you know.
It's not in the evening like we eat here.
And I remember-- [LAUGHS] this was the first time.
This is sort of a rude thing.
I remember we were finished eating, and I burped.
I did not know what part of my body this sound came from or what it meant.
We were all a little more naive than today's children.
And the people about dropped their fork and knife right out of their hand.
And they said, what do you say?
And I said, I don't know.
What-- what is it?
What-- what's it about?
And they said, but one does not burp at the table.
That's the first time I knew the word 'burp' even.
Had apparently not had the necessity to burp.

And the next place they parked me-- because I had to get away from there, although that man had bought me nice clothes because he thought I was such a perfect specimen of a German child with the blue eyes and the hair.
This was [GERMAN]?
[GERMAN]. 'Froh' means 'glad.' So she had a very sunny disposition.
That's probably why they called her that.
I think her name was Gerda.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The next person I stayed with briefly was apparently a-- a doctor, a woman doctor who practiced out of her house.
And she couldn't take heathens, so I had to be baptized before she would let me stay there.
And Hilde said, yes, go ahead, baptize, whatever it takes.
And during this time you had no contact with your parents?
Mm-mm, mm-mm, mm-mm.
They heard about me, I know that.

[DRINKS] But-- and it didn't occur to me-- as long as Hilde said, this is what you're going to do and here's where you're going, I-- I had no sense to be afraid or to think, won't they ever come back?
I was-- if you're two years old-- you know, I sat at the door at age two at my grandmother's before she died and cried till my parents came back.
And at that age, you just do what some have-- it's explained, it's adequate.
You're neither happy nor sad, neither one.
Well, I remember Hilde took me on the streetcar to get to some other place.
And I remember she sat down and I said, Tante Hilde, how come you're sitting down?
We're not allowed to sit down on the streetcar.

I mean, there were rituals that we had done that we took for granted because we had to do them because of who we were that now were lifted by my being with another person, and I'm noticing the difference.
And she-- she said-- years later, she said, do you remember that episode?
I said, oh, yes.
Because she was incapable of anger, this woman.
She was so kind and still is.
The onlookers in the streetcar clicked right here.
See, the Germans were trained to recognize and listen and observe and denounce everything they saw and heard, and even things they didn't see and hear.
She knew that some of the people on that streetcar knew what this meant.
She had to get off.
Immediately we had to get off.
And on a trip to the milk store-- in those times, each store was separate with what you needed pretty much-- I said, how come you're buying milk?
We can't buy milk.
See, this was dangerous stuff.
If my parent had been there, or one of them at least, I would have been told to keep my mouth closed and wouldn't be allowed to talk.
So we had to leave the milk store very fast also.
We left a number of places fast.
Or when the air raids came, if I happened to be with her, which was more often, that was always the station to come back to.
And on days when I wasn't there, apparently my mother was there, but I didn't know that.
Again, the-- there's a concierge-type person in the downstairs who takes note of every coming and going, and how long did they stay, and what are their habits?
And it's their responsibility, you know?
And you have to fill out a form if you stay there a couple of days.
You can't get a hotel room without filling it out in detail with your-- your identification card and so on.
My mother pretended to water the flowers at her place while Hilde would be gone, because she often went to another city in connection with her studies.
And my mother would stay in the apartment.
But when air raids came, it was a big drama.
When Hilde and I were in the apartment-- and my mother was, god knows, with whom elsewhere living-- she and I would go down to the air raid shelter.
And I remember she would put me on her lap and say, [GERMAN], sleep.
And I would put my head there, and somehow I went to sleep on automatic.
You just have to do what you have to do.
Ah, no.
That was very brief.
The baptism didn't help me a whole lot there.
One of the things that upset the woman immediately, she had some other children there in the daytime.
And I don't know if they were her own or what the devil.
In fact, some of my photographs were taken on her balcony in clothes she provided and toys that she had provided by virtue of having them already.
It must have been her children.
Don't know, my mother doesn't know, nobody knows.
Hilde doesn't know.
She doesn't remember who the person was, only that it was a woman doctor.
She called Hilde on the second day and said, can't keep this child.
This child prays in some funny gibberish stuff.
There's something not right here.
And she was a devout Christian, which is why she couldn't have a heathen in her house and had to baptize me, however she did that, or whatever constituted baptism to her.
And I had been taught a night prayer, and that-- that was about all I knew.
And I wasn't saying the, God preserve me and Jesus look over me and so forth.
I learned that later at another place.
But we had to get rid of that.
See, these are small details.
Who could think of all these things?
I certainly didn't understand what I was saying and what it was for.
That was what I did.
So I had to be moved from there real fast.
I don't know with how many people I stayed.
I have no idea.
No one really knows, because sometimes one would take me to the-- the next.
I became known to the parents of some of the university women students, like [GERMAN], as the illegitimate child of another student who didn't want anyone to know that she'd had a baby.
But in those times, Hitler much advocated procreation.
Strength through pleasure-- [SPEAKING GERMAN], which was to say if you're a sturdy German woman, what you need to do is reproduce yourself so we have more people as perfect as you.
So that wasn't too bad.
The parents were perhaps in shock, but otherwise no one else.
